gpt-5.5.geometry_accuracy_analysis: The scene contains three true 3D height-field surface panels: a positive Gaussian mound, a Gaussian-windowed cosine carrier, and a Gaussian-windowed sine/quadrature carrier, arranged side by side with mesh/grid overlays and labels (a), (b), and (c). The surfaces are constructed from symmetric x–z grids with y displacement, so depth is generated by actual 3D geometry and camera projection rather than a flat pasted or skewed image. Major geometric content is present and recognizable, though fine textbook details such as exact viewing angle, axes/base styling, and precise proportions may not perfectly match the source.
gpt-5.5.geometry_accuracy_reasoning: All major Gabor-function surface elements are represented as real 3D geometry with plausible arrangement and shapes, with only minor likely discrepancies in styling, viewpoint, or proportional details.
gpt-5.5.interactivity_usability_analysis: The code includes OrbitControls for drag/zoom, but this does not count as a meaningful interaction. A visible u0 range slider updates the frequency scale of the cosine/sine Gabor surfaces in real time and shows a popup on change explaining the parameter; this visibly functions and is pedagogically relevant. The three surface panels are registered as interactive objects: pointer hover changes the cursor and shows a tooltip, and clicking a panel opens an explanatory popup and highlights the selected panel’s grid/outline; this is functional and useful. A reset-view button has a click handler but is hidden with display:none, so it is not usable. The UI is very compact in the top-left corner and does not obscure the geometry.
gpt-5.5.interactivity_usability_reasoning: There are two meaningful functional interactions—the u0 slider and click/hover explanatory panel selection—with compact nonintrusive UI, but no guided demo or third substantial interaction, so this fits score 4.

gpt-5.5.concept_accuracy_analysis: The figure illustrates three Gabor-related surfaces: a Gaussian window, a cosine-phase Gabor function, and a sine/quadrature-phase Gabor function, with a u0 slider changing the sinusoidal carrier frequency while the Gaussian envelope remains fixed. The popups are figure-specific and mostly correct, explicitly describing the Gaussian envelope, cosine carrier with central positive ridge, sine-phase zero crossing, and central frequency parameter; only minor notation inconsistency appears because the surface uses x and z coordinates while the popup writes x and y.
